ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for spicy chicken tenders as a prepared dish; SNOMED CT coverage for composite fast-food items is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of 3 pieces of spicy chicken tenders weighs approximately 150 g, providing around 407 kcal, 28.8 g protein, 21.0 g fat, and 25.8 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Spicy Chicken Tenders — Singapore HPB Foodo Database; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #05062 for fatty acid and vitamin cross-validation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore at confidence 0.95 for the named product 'Spicy Chicken Tenders'. Saturated fat (3.5 g/100g), trans fat (0.15 g/100g), and vitamin C (0.0 mg/100g) were not available in HPB Foodo for this product; these values were derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #05062 (Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, meat only, fried, coated), which is an approved secondary source. The original input record attributed these three values to 'grabfood', which is a forbidden delivery-platform source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Those attributions have been replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the approved evidence basis. All retained values are consistent with the HPB macro profile and plausible for a deep-fried breaded chicken product.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate for fried breaded chicken products (GI ~65, medium category).
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for spicy chicken tenders as a named product in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI of 65 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables, which report a medium GI range of 60–70 for fried battered and breaded chicken products, attributable to the refined wheat flour and corn starch coating. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (65 × 25.8) / 100 = 16.8, based on the 150 g default serving containing 25.8 g carbohydrate (17.2 g carb/100g × 1.5).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 769 mg/100g — well above the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should avoid or strictly limit spicy chicken tenders; a single 150 g serving delivers approximately 1,154 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the 2,000 mg daily sodium target recommended by the Singapore HPB for hypertensive adults.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate coating (17.2 g carb/100g, low fibre 1.2 g/100g) with deep-fried preparation creates a glycaemic load of 16.8 per standard 150 g serving at an estimated GI of 65
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit consumption of spicy chicken tenders due to the rapidly digestible refined wheat flour and corn starch batter (estimated GI 65, GL 16.8 per 150 g serving), which can cause significant post-prandial blood glucose elevation; if consumed, pair with non-starchy vegetables and limit portion to 3 pieces maximum.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined burden of high sodium (769 mg/100g), saturated fat (3.5 g/100g), trans fat (0.15 g/100g), and deep-fried preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of spicy chicken tenders; the combination of trans fat (0.15 g/100g), saturated fat (3.5 g/100g), and very high sodium (769 mg/100g) adversely affects LDL cholesterol, endothelial function, and blood pressure simultaneously, compounding cardiovascular risk per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and trans fat (0.15 g/100g) is strongly pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations such as spicy chicken tenders; AGE formation during deep-frying and the presence of trans fat directly exacerbate joint inflammation per EULAR dietary guidelines, and air-frying or oven-baking with reduced sodium seasoning is strongly preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Chicken Tenders are boneless strips of chicken breast fillet coated in a seasoned batter of wheat flour, corn starch, chilli powder, paprika, garlic powder, and black pepper, then deep-fried until golden and crispy. The exterior is crunchy and well-spiced with moderate heat, while the interior remains juicy and tender. This dish is a Western fast-food staple widely available in Singapore through quick-service restaurants and food delivery platforms, typically served in portions of 3 or 5 pieces. Per 100 g, the dish provides 271 kcal, 19.2 g protein, 14.0 g fat (3.5 g saturated, 0.15 g trans), and 17.2 g carbohydrate with only 1.2 g dietary fibre. Sodium content is very high at 769 mg/100g, making it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension. The refined carbohydrate coating and deep-fried preparation result in a medium glycaemic index (estimated GI 65) and a glycaemic load of 16.8 per standard 150 g serving, posing a meaningful post-prandial glucose challenge for people with type 2 diabetes. The trans fat content and saturated fat burden also raise cardiovascular concern. Clinically, this food is rated high concern for hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. It is halal-certified in Singapore commercial contexts and contains gluten allergens from wheat flour. Common serving sizes are 3 pieces (approximately 150 g) or 5 pieces (approximately 200 g).

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore at confidence 0.95 for an exact named product match. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #05062 (fried coated chicken breast), which is an approved secondary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed international GI tables for fried breaded chicken products.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this specific spicy chicken tender product not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #05062 as of 2026-04-16. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Foodo for this product. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data beyond those reported in HPB Foodo not available for this product in approved sources as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #05062 for comparable fried coated chicken products; consistent with a deep-fried meat product containing no vegetable components. Two portions are listed as is_default true in the source data (3pc and 5pc); the 3-piece 150 g portion is used as the primary serving reference for GL calculation. The original input record listed 'grabfood' as a source for saturated_fat, trans_fat, and vitamin_c; GrabFood is a delivery platform and is a forbidden primary source per Project Veritas v2 source policy. These values have been re-attributed to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #05062 as the approved evidence basis after cross-validation confirmed consistency with the HPB macro profile.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index 65 (medium), gi_category medium, glycemic_load_per_serving 16.8. gi_source now explicitly cites Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care, peer-reviewed journal) and the University of Sydney GI Database as the analogous estimate basis for fried breaded chicken products. Estimation basis and GL calculation fully documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03302051 and label 'chicken tender (food product)' present. FoodEx2 code A0CKN for 'Chicken meat, coated and fried' is defensible. SNOMED CT null with documented gap note. No invented codes.

Covers preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context (Singapore fast food), clinical relevance (hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease), and key nutrients.

Each has concern_level, key_factor, and a specific clinical guidance sentence.

Comprehensive coverage for a Western fast-food dish served in Singapore.
